title: Module: bias, confounding & conflicts of interest
context: Bias, confounding, and conflicts of interest are pervasive in oncology research and are the hardest for advocates to spot. This module provides a structured taxonomy of bias types (selection, performance, detection, attrition, reporting), explains confounding in plain language, and gives advocates a framework for identifying and weighting conflicts of interest and funding sources. It is patient-facing educational content requiring dual high-tier sign-off.
objective: Author an original, plain-language educational module on bias types, confounding, and conflicts of interest in oncology research — providing advocates with a structured framework for identifying and interpreting these threats to evidence quality.
acceptanceCriteria: Covers the major bias types relevant to oncology RCTs and observational studies: selection bias, performance bias, detection bias, attrition bias, and reporting bias (including outcome reporting bias and publication bias), with plain-language definitions and examples.
Explains confounding in plain language: what it is, why it matters, and how to identify methods that attempt to control for it (randomisation, adjustment, restriction, propensity scoring).

Covers conflicts of interest and funding: types of financial and non-financial COI, how to locate COI disclosures, what industry funding is associated with in the oncology literature, and the advocate's role in noting (not adjudicating) COI.
Cites authoritative sources (Cochrane RoB tool methodology, GRADE for reporting bias, COI research); all citations resolve to sources.yaml with compatible allowedUse.
Written in plain language; no unexplained jargon without a glossary anchor.
Carries a persistent 'education only — not medical advice' notice and passes the advice-language checklist (task 002).
Passes the licence + provenance build gate and CI.
Signed off by a credentialed oncologist (recorded in the review ledger): clinical framing is correct and nothing reads as treatment guidance.
Signed off by an experienced patient advocate (recorded in the review ledger): content is accessible and does not stray into advice.
Signed off by a methodology reviewer: bias taxonomy and confounding explanations are methodologically accurate.
resources: PLAN.md (§ Goals — Goal 2; § Risks — methodological error)
https://training.cochrane.org/handbook (Cochrane Handbook — RoB tool and bias taxonomy)
output: A plain-language educational module (content/modules/bias-confounding-coi.md) committed to the repo, covering bias types, confounding, and conflicts of interest, provenance-cited, CI-green, and carrying dual high-tier sign-off plus methodology review.
